sources.note: Stems/options scraped verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e Review Questions. OpenStax does NOT publish the Biology 2e review-question answer key publicly, so correctAnswer is verified-from-source-text (bedrock factual/computational items cross-checked against the Biology 2e chapter text). NOTE on license: Biology 2e was originally released as CC BY 4.0 (still cited that way by some mirrors and by this repo's seed questions.json); OpenStax's current official listing for Biology 2e is CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, recorded here as the conservative/authoritative value. OpenStax also posts a 'no LLM training' notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing per PRD.

meta.notes: File 06: OpenStax Biology 2e Ch. 10 (Cell Reproduction / cell cycle & mitosis) and Ch. 11 (Meiosis & Sexual Reproduction). One figure-referencing item (Ch.10 Q15, 'Figure 10.6') was skipped. Options 1-4 map to A-D. answerProvenance is verified-from-source-text for all items (no public OpenStax key).

questions.stem: A diploid cell has _______ the number of chromosomes as a haploid cell.
questions.explanation: A diploid cell (2n) contains two sets of chromosomes, which is twice the number found in a haploid cell (n).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q3). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text (definition of diploid vs haploid).

hints.prompt: How many chromosome sets does a diploid cell have compared with a haploid cell?
hints.rationale: A diploid cell (2n) has two chromosome sets; a haploid cell (n) has one.

hints.prompt: Is a diploid cell's chromosome number smaller than or larger than a haploid cell's?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Larger, because it has an extra full set

choices.label: B
choices.text: Smaller, because it has fewer sets

hints.rationale: Diploid cells have more chromosomes than haploid cells, so 'half' and 'one-fourth' are wrong.

hints.prompt: Why is 'half' the reverse of the correct relationship?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A haploid cell has half a diploid cell's chromosomes, so diploid is not half of haploid

choices.label: B
choices.text: A diploid cell has half as many chromosomes as a haploid cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Half' correctly describes diploid relative to haploid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Haploid means double the chromosome number

hints.rationale: Half describes haploid relative to diploid, the opposite of what the stem asks.

questions.stem: An organism's traits are determined by the specific combination of inherited _____.
questions.explanation: Heritable traits are determined by the combination of genes (alleles) an organism inherits from its parents.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q4). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: What are the basic units of heredity that determine an organism's traits?
hints.rationale: Genes are the heritable units that determine traits.

hints.prompt: Are cells and proteins the inherited units themselves, or products of gene expression?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Products of gene expression, not the inherited units

choices.label: B
choices.text: The inherited units of heredity themselves

choices.label: C
choices.text: Identical to genes in every way

hints.rationale: Cells and proteins result from genes; the inherited units are the genes.

hints.prompt: Why are 'chromatids' not the best answer for the inherited units that determine traits?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chromatids are copies of a chromosome during division; the trait-determining heritable units are genes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chromatids are the fundamental units of heredity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Genes are built out of chromatids

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chromatids are a type of protein

hints.rationale: A chromatid is a chromosome copy, not the heritable unit that specifies a trait.

questions.stem: The first level of DNA organization in a eukaryotic cell is maintained by which molecule?
questions.explanation: The first level of DNA packing is the wrapping of DNA around histone proteins to form nucleosomes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q5). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text (DNA winds around histones as the first packing level).

hints.prompt: The first level of eukaryotic DNA packing is the winding of DNA around what?
hints.rationale: DNA first wraps around histones to form nucleosomes.

hints.prompt: Cohesin and condensin are proteins that mainly act when, rather than forming the first packing level?

choices.label: A
choices.text: During chromosome cohesion and condensation in cell division

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only during DNA's first level of packing at rest

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only in the cytoplasm during translation

hints.rationale: Cohesin and condensin work in division-related cohesion/condensation, not the first packing level.

hints.prompt: Why is 'chromatin' not the molecule that maintains the first packing level?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chromatin is the overall DNA-plus-protein complex, not the specific protein DNA first wraps around

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chromatin is a single protein DNA winds around

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chromatin is just another word for a histone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chromatin is an enzyme that packs DNA

hints.rationale: Chromatin is the whole complex; the protein DNA first wraps around is the histone.

questions.stem: Identical copies of chromatin held together by cohesin at the centromere are called _____.
questions.explanation: After DNA replication, the two identical copies of a chromosome held together at the centromere by cohesin are sister chromatids.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q6). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: After a chromosome is replicated, how many identical copies exist joined together?
hints.rationale: Replication produces two identical joined copies of the chromosome.

hints.prompt: Are histones and nucleosomes the two joined identical copies, or DNA-packing components?

choices.label: A
choices.text: DNA-packing components, not the identical copies themselves

choices.label: B
choices.text: The two identical joined chromosome copies

hints.rationale: Histones and nucleosomes package DNA; they are not the paired identical copies.

hints.prompt: Why is 'chromatin' not the term for the two identical joined copies?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chromatin is the general DNA-protein material, not the paired identical copies joined at the centromere

choices.label: B
choices.text: Chromatin means exactly two joined copies

choices.label: C
choices.text: Chromatin is what cohesin holds together as a pair

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chromatin is a single one of the copies

hints.rationale: Chromatin refers to the DNA-protein material broadly, not the paired copies.

questions.stem: Chromosomes are duplicated during what stage of the cell cycle?
questions.explanation: DNA replication, which duplicates the chromosomes, occurs during the S (synthesis) phase of interphase.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q7). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: During which part of the cell cycle is a cell's DNA replicated?
hints.rationale: DNA replication occurs in the S phase.

hints.prompt: The 'S' in S phase stands for what?
hints.rationale: S phase is the DNA synthesis phase, when chromosomes are copied.

hints.prompt: Why is 'prophase' not when chromosomes are duplicated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prophase is a mitotic stage where already-duplicated chromosomes condense, not when they are copied

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prophase is when DNA synthesis happens

choices.label: C
choices.text: Duplication occurs in prophase rather than interphase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prophase is a part of interphase

hints.rationale: By prophase the DNA is already copied; duplication happened earlier in S phase.

questions.stem: Which of the following events does not occur during some stages of interphase?
questions.explanation: Sister chromatids separate during mitosis (anaphase), not interphase. DNA and organelle duplication and cell growth all occur during interphase.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q8). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Which activities are hallmarks of interphase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: DNA replication, organelle duplication, and cell growth

choices.label: D
choices.text: Alignment of chromosomes at the metaphase plate

hints.rationale: Interphase includes growth, DNA replication, and organelle duplication.

hints.prompt: Do DNA duplication and organelle duplication occur during interphase?

choices.label: B
choices.text: No; both occur only during mitosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only organelle duplication occurs in interphase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neither occurs in the cell cycle

hints.rationale: Because both happen in interphase, they are not the event that fails to occur then.

hints.prompt: Why is 'increase in cell size' NOT the correct choice (since it does occur during interphase)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cell growth is a normal interphase activity, so it is not the event excluded from interphase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Growth is the same event as chromatid separation

hints.rationale: Cell growth is part of interphase, so it is not the excluded event.

questions.stem: The mitotic spindles arise from which cell structure?
questions.explanation: The centrosome is the microtubule-organizing center from which the mitotic spindle fibers emanate.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q9). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Which structure serves as the microtubule-organizing center from which spindle fibers emanate?
hints.rationale: The centrosome organizes the mitotic spindle.

hints.prompt: The similarly named 'centromere' is a region of the chromosome; does it generate spindle fibers?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; it is where kinetochores form for attachment, not where spindles originate

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; it is the source of all spindle fibers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; it is the microtubule-organizing center

hints.rationale: The centromere is an attachment site on the chromosome, not the spindle's origin.

hints.prompt: Why is the 'kinetochore' not where mitotic spindles arise?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The kinetochore is a protein complex on the centromere where spindle fibers attach, not where they originate

choices.label: B
choices.text: The kinetochore generates all spindle fibers

choices.label: C
choices.text: The kinetochore is the microtubule-organizing center

choices.label: D
choices.text: The kinetochore is identical to the centrosome

hints.rationale: Kinetochores are attachment points for spindles, not the site the spindles come from.

questions.stem: Attachment of the mitotic spindle fibers to the kinetochores is a characteristic of which stage of mitosis?
questions.explanation: During prometaphase the nuclear envelope breaks down and spindle microtubules attach to the kinetochores of the chromosomes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q10). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: During which stage does the nuclear envelope break down and spindle fibers attach to kinetochores?
hints.rationale: Prometaphase features envelope breakdown and spindle-kinetochore attachment.

hints.prompt: During the preceding prophase, what are the chromosomes mainly doing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Condensing while the nuclear envelope is still largely intact

choices.label: B
choices.text: Already fully attached to spindle fibers

choices.label: C
choices.text: Being pulled apart to the poles

hints.rationale: In prophase chromosomes condense before attachment, so attachment is not yet complete.

hints.prompt: Why is 'metaphase' not when spindle fibers FIRST attach to kinetochores?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By metaphase, attachment has already occurred and chromosomes are aligned at the metaphase plate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Metaphase is when the nuclear envelope first breaks down

choices.label: D
choices.text: No spindle attachment exists during metaphase

hints.rationale: Attachment happens in prometaphase; by metaphase the chromosomes are already attached and aligned.

questions.stem: Unpacking of chromosomes and the formation of a new nuclear envelope is a characteristic of which stage of mitosis?
questions.explanation: During telophase the chromosomes decondense (unpack) and a new nuclear envelope forms around each set of chromosomes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q11). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: During which mitotic stage do chromosomes decondense and new nuclear envelopes form?
hints.rationale: Telophase features chromosome decondensation and nuclear envelope reformation.

hints.prompt: Telophase essentially reverses which earlier stage (condensation and envelope breakdown)?
hints.rationale: Telophase undoes the condensation and envelope breakdown of early mitosis.

hints.prompt: Why is 'anaphase' not when the nuclear envelope reforms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anaphase is when sister chromatids are pulled to opposite poles, before the envelope reforms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anaphase is when new envelopes form around chromosomes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anaphase is when chromosomes fully decondense

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anaphase is the final mitotic stage

hints.rationale: Envelope reformation occurs in telophase; anaphase is the earlier chromatid-separation stage.

questions.stem: Separation of the sister chromatids is a characteristic of which stage of mitosis?
questions.explanation: Sister chromatids are pulled apart toward opposite poles during anaphase.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q12). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: During which stage are sister chromatids pulled toward opposite poles?
hints.rationale: Sister chromatids separate during anaphase.

hints.prompt: At metaphase, are the sister chromatids separated yet?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; they are aligned at the metaphase plate and still joined

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; they have fully separated by metaphase

hints.rationale: Chromatids remain joined and aligned at metaphase, separating only afterward.

hints.prompt: Why is 'telophase' not when sister chromatids separate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By telophase the chromatids have already separated and reached the poles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Telophase is when chromatids first split apart

hints.rationale: Separation happens in anaphase; by telophase it is already complete.

questions.stem: The chromosomes become visible under a light microscope during which stage of mitosis?
questions.explanation: During prophase the chromatin condenses into discrete chromosomes that become visible under a light microscope.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q13). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: During which stage does chromatin condense into chromosomes visible under a light microscope?
hints.rationale: Chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes during prophase.

hints.prompt: Before prophase, DNA exists as diffuse chromatin. Is it visible as distinct chromosomes then?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; it is too dispersed to see as distinct chromosomes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; individual chromosomes are clearly visible

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only under an electron microscope during S phase

hints.rationale: Diffuse chromatin is not visible as distinct chromosomes, so condensation must occur first.

hints.prompt: Why is 'metaphase' not the FIRST stage chromosomes become visible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chromosomes are already visible by metaphase; they first become visible earlier, in prophase

hints.rationale: Chromosomes become visible in prophase, before the metaphase alignment stage.

questions.stem: The fusing of Golgi vesicles at the metaphase plate of dividing plant cells forms what structure?
questions.explanation: In plant cytokinesis, Golgi-derived vesicles fuse at the center of the cell to form the cell plate, which becomes the new cell wall.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q14). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Plant cells divide their cytoplasm by building which structure across the middle?
hints.rationale: Plant cytokinesis forms a cell plate that becomes the new wall.

hints.prompt: How do animal cells, lacking a rigid wall, instead divide the cytoplasm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By pinching inward with a cleavage furrow

hints.rationale: Animal cells use a cleavage furrow, unlike the plant cell plate.

hints.prompt: Why is the 'cleavage furrow' not how plant cells complete division?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A cleavage furrow pinches animal cells, but rigid plant cell walls require building a cell plate instead

choices.label: B
choices.text: Plant cells pinch inward with a cleavage furrow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cleavage furrows build the new plant cell wall

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plant and animal cytokinesis are identical

hints.rationale: Rigid plant walls cannot pinch, so plants use a cell plate rather than a furrow.

questions.stem: At which of the cell-cycle checkpoints do external forces have the greatest influence?
questions.explanation: The G1 checkpoint (restriction point) is where external signals such as growth factors most strongly influence whether the cell commits to division.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q16). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: External signals such as growth factors most strongly influence the decision to divide at which checkpoint?
hints.rationale: The G1 checkpoint is where external growth signals most influence commitment to divide.

hints.prompt: The G2 checkpoint mainly verifies which INTERNAL condition, rather than responding to external growth signals?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That DNA has been completely and accurately replicated

choices.label: B
choices.text: That growth factors are present outside the cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: That the cell has finished mitosis

hints.rationale: G2 checks internal DNA replication, not external growth cues.

hints.prompt: Why is the 'M checkpoint' not the one most influenced by external forces?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The M (spindle) checkpoint monitors internal spindle attachment, not external growth signals

choices.label: B
choices.text: The M checkpoint responds mainly to outside growth factors

choices.label: C
choices.text: The M checkpoint is the restriction point

choices.label: D
choices.text: The M checkpoint controls entry into S phase

hints.rationale: The M checkpoint watches internal spindle attachment, not outside signals.

questions.stem: What is the main prerequisite for clearance at the G2 checkpoint?

choices.label: A
choices.text: cell has reached a sufficient size

choices.label: D
choices.text: proper attachment of mitotic spindle fibers to kinetochores

questions.explanation: The G2 checkpoint verifies that DNA has been accurately and completely replicated (and any damage repaired) before mitosis begins.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q17). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Just before mitosis, the G2 checkpoint checks that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: DNA has been accurately and completely replicated

choices.label: B
choices.text: The cell has already finished mitosis

hints.rationale: G2 verifies complete, accurate DNA replication before mitosis.

hints.prompt: The G2 checkpoint comes right after which phase, whose success it confirms?
hints.rationale: G2 follows S phase and confirms DNA replication succeeded.

hints.prompt: Why is 'proper attachment of spindle fibers to kinetochores' not the G2 prerequisite?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spindle-kinetochore attachment is checked at the later M checkpoint, not at G2

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the main thing the G2 checkpoint verifies

choices.label: D
choices.text: G2 occurs in the middle of mitosis

hints.rationale: Spindle attachment is monitored at the M checkpoint, not G2.

questions.stem: If the M checkpoint is not cleared, what stage of mitosis will be blocked?
questions.explanation: The M (spindle) checkpoint, at the metaphase-to-anaphase transition, blocks entry into anaphase until all kinetochores are properly attached to the spindle.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q18). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: The M (spindle) checkpoint controls the transition into which stage?
hints.rationale: The M checkpoint gates the metaphase-to-anaphase transition.

hints.prompt: The M checkpoint ensures all kinetochores are attached before allowing chromatids to do what?
hints.rationale: It permits chromatid separation (anaphase) only when attachment is complete.

hints.prompt: Why is 'prophase' not the stage blocked by an unmet M checkpoint?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prophase occurs before the metaphase-to-anaphase checkpoint, so it is not what gets blocked

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prophase is triggered by the M checkpoint

choices.label: C
choices.text: The M checkpoint blocks entry into prophase

hints.rationale: Prophase happens earlier, so the M checkpoint does not block it.

questions.stem: Which protein is a positive regulator that phosphorylates other proteins when activated?
questions.explanation: Cyclin-dependent kinases (Cdks) are positive regulators that, when activated by binding cyclin, phosphorylate target proteins to drive the cell cycle forward.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q19). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: An enzyme that phosphorylates (adds phosphate groups to) other proteins is a:
hints.rationale: Kinases phosphorylate target proteins.

hints.prompt: Are p53 and Rb positive 'go' regulators or negative 'brake' regulators of the cell cycle?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive regulators that drive the cycle forward

hints.rationale: p53 and Rb are negative regulators, not the positive phosphorylating driver.

hints.prompt: Why is 'cyclin' not the phosphorylating enzyme itself?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cyclin is a regulatory partner that activates a Cdk; the Cdk is the enzyme that phosphorylates targets

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cyclin is the kinase that adds phosphate groups

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cyclin removes phosphate groups from proteins

hints.rationale: Cyclin activates Cdk but does not itself phosphorylate targets.

questions.stem: Many of the negative regulator proteins of the cell cycle were discovered in what type of cells?
questions.explanation: Negative cell-cycle regulators (tumor suppressors) were largely identified through studying cancer cells, in which these brakes are defective.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q20). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Negative cell-cycle regulators act as brakes. They were largely discovered by studying cells in which these brakes fail, namely:
hints.rationale: Tumor suppressors were mainly identified through cancer cells with defective brakes.

hints.prompt: In cancer cells, the negative regulators (brakes) are typically:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Defective or lost, allowing uncontrolled division

choices.label: D
choices.text: Replaced by extra positive regulators only

hints.rationale: Cancer cells have faulty brakes, which is why they revealed these regulators.

hints.prompt: Why are 'gametes' not where negative regulators were mainly discovered?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Gametes are normal reproductive cells; the brake defects that revealed these regulators are seen in cancer cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gametes have the most defective checkpoints

hints.rationale: Gametes are normal cells, so they did not reveal defective brakes the way cancer cells did.

questions.stem: Which negative regulatory molecule can trigger cell suicide (apoptosis) if vital cell cycle events do not occur?
questions.explanation: p53 halts the cell cycle for repair and can trigger apoptosis if DNA damage is too severe to fix.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q21). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Which 'guardian' protein can halt the cell cycle for repair and trigger apoptosis if damage is too severe?
hints.rationale: p53 arrests the cycle and can trigger apoptosis when damage is irreparable.

hints.prompt: Cyclin and Cdk are positive 'go' regulators; can they act as brakes that trigger cell suicide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; they drive the cycle forward rather than trigger apoptosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; they are the main apoptosis triggers

choices.label: D
choices.text: They have no role in the cell cycle

hints.rationale: Cyclin/Cdk push the cycle forward, so they are not the apoptosis-triggering brakes.

hints.prompt: Why is 'retinoblastoma protein (Rb)' not the apoptosis trigger described?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rb restrains the cycle at the G1 restriction point but is not the guardian that triggers apoptosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rb is the main inducer of apoptosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rb is a positive regulator that speeds the cycle

hints.rationale: Rb is a brake at G1, whereas the apoptosis-triggering guardian described is a different protein.

questions.stem: ___________ are changes to the order of nucleotides in a segment of DNA that codes for a protein.
questions.explanation: Gene mutations are changes in the nucleotide sequence of a DNA segment that codes for a protein.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q22). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: A change in the sequence of nucleotides in DNA is called a:
hints.rationale: A change in nucleotide sequence is a mutation.

hints.prompt: Are proto-oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes themselves 'changes to a nucleotide sequence'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; they are genes, not changes to the sequence

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; both are definitions of a sequence change

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; a proto-oncogene means a mutation

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are not related to DNA at all

hints.rationale: Those are categories of genes, not the term for a sequence change.

hints.prompt: Why are 'negative regulators' not the correct term for changes in nucleotide sequence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Negative regulators are proteins that brake the cycle, not changes to a DNA sequence

choices.label: B
choices.text: Negative regulators are a kind of DNA sequence change

choices.label: C
choices.text: A mutation is a type of negative regulator

choices.label: D
choices.text: Negative regulator and mutation mean the same thing

hints.rationale: Negative regulators are proteins, not sequence alterations.

questions.stem: A gene that codes for a positive cell-cycle regulator is called a(n) _____.
questions.explanation: A normal gene encoding a positive cell-cycle regulator is a proto-oncogene; a cancer-promoting mutated version is an oncogene.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q23). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: A normal gene that codes for a positive (go) cell-cycle regulator is a:
hints.rationale: A normal gene for a positive regulator is a proto-oncogene.

hints.prompt: Does a tumor suppressor gene code for a positive (go) or a negative (brake) regulator?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A positive regulator that drives division

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neither; it codes for a structural protein

hints.rationale: Tumor suppressors encode negative regulators, so they are not the 'positive regulator' gene.

hints.prompt: Why is 'oncogene' not the term for the NORMAL gene encoding a positive regulator?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An oncogene is the cancer-causing mutated form; the normal version is a proto-oncogene

choices.label: B
choices.text: An oncogene is the normal, healthy version of the gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oncogene and proto-oncogene mean exactly the same thing

choices.label: D
choices.text: An oncogene is a brake gene

hints.rationale: The normal gene is a proto-oncogene; an oncogene is its mutated, cancer-driving form.

questions.stem: A mutated gene that codes for an altered version of Cdk that is active in the absence of cyclin is a(n) _____.
questions.explanation: A mutation that makes Cdk constitutively active (independent of cyclin) drives uncontrolled division; such a cancer-promoting mutant gene is an oncogene.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q24). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: A mutated gene that promotes uncontrolled cell division (cancer) is a(n):
hints.rationale: A cancer-promoting mutated gene is an oncogene.

hints.prompt: A Cdk that is active even WITHOUT cyclin pushes the cycle forward improperly. Is that a gain of a 'go' signal or a loss of a brake?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A gain of an overactive 'go' signal

choices.label: B
choices.text: A loss of a braking (tumor-suppressor) signal

choices.label: C
choices.text: No change to the cycle at all

choices.label: D
choices.text: A repair of a damaged gene

hints.rationale: Constitutively active Cdk is an overactive go signal, the behavior of an oncogene.

hints.prompt: Why is 'proto-oncogene' not the best answer for this already-mutated, constantly-active Cdk gene?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A proto-oncogene is the normal unmutated form; once mutated to be constantly active it becomes an oncogene

choices.label: B
choices.text: A proto-oncogene is the mutated, cancer-driving form

hints.rationale: The mutated, constitutively active version is an oncogene, not the normal proto-oncogene.

questions.stem: Which molecule is a Cdk inhibitor that is controlled by p53?
questions.explanation: p53 induces expression of p21, which inhibits Cdk activity and halts cell-cycle progression.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q25). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: When p53 halts the cell cycle, it switches on which Cdk-inhibiting protein?
hints.rationale: p53 induces p21, which inhibits Cdk activity.

hints.prompt: Cyclin's role is to ACTIVATE Cdk; could cyclin be the Cdk INHIBITOR asked for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; cyclin activates Cdk, the opposite of inhibiting it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; cyclin is the main Cdk inhibitor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cyclin has no effect on Cdk

hints.rationale: Cyclin activates Cdk, so it cannot be the inhibitor.

hints.prompt: Why is 'Rb' not the p53-controlled Cdk inhibitor described?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rb restrains the cycle by binding transcription factors, but the Cdk inhibitor induced by p53 is p21

choices.label: B
choices.text: Rb is directly switched on by p53 to inhibit Cdk

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rb is a type of cyclin

hints.rationale: Rb acts through transcription factors; the p53-induced Cdk inhibitor is p21.

questions.stem: Which eukaryotic cell-cycle event is missing in binary fission?
questions.explanation: Prokaryotes lack a nucleus and a mitotic apparatus, so binary fission has no karyokinesis (nuclear division). They still grow, replicate DNA, and divide the cytoplasm.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q26). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Prokaryotes have no nucleus, so binary fission lacks which event?
hints.rationale: Without a nucleus, binary fission has no karyokinesis.

hints.prompt: Do prokaryotes still replicate their DNA and grow before dividing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes; they replicate DNA and grow, then divide

choices.label: B
choices.text: No; they skip DNA replication entirely

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only if they have a nucleus

hints.rationale: Prokaryotes do replicate DNA and grow, so those are not the missing events.

hints.prompt: Why is 'cytokinesis' NOT the event missing in binary fission?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Binary fission still divides the cytoplasm (cytokinesis) into two cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Binary fission skips dividing the cytoplasm

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cytokinesis requires a nucleus to occur

hints.rationale: Binary fission does divide the cytoplasm, so cytokinesis is present.

questions.stem: FtsZ proteins direct the formation of a _______ that will eventually form the new cell walls of the daughter cells.
questions.explanation: In bacterial binary fission, FtsZ proteins assemble at midcell and direct formation of the septum, which becomes the new cell walls separating the daughter cells.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 10 Cell Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.10 review Q27). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: In bacterial binary fission, FtsZ directs formation of the dividing wall known as the:
hints.rationale: FtsZ directs septum formation, which becomes the new cell walls.

hints.prompt: The 'cell plate' is the dividing structure in which organisms, making it wrong for bacteria?
hints.rationale: Cell plates form in plant cells, not bacteria.

hints.prompt: Why is 'contractile ring' not the structure that becomes the new bacterial cell walls?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The FtsZ ring constricts, but the structure that actually forms the new cell walls is the septum it builds

choices.label: B
choices.text: The contractile ring itself becomes the cell wall

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bacteria build a cell plate rather than a septum

choices.label: D
choices.text: The septum forms only in animal cells

hints.rationale: The FtsZ ring drives constriction, while the septum it directs becomes the new walls.

questions.stem: Meiosis usually produces ________ daughter cells.
questions.explanation: Meiosis comprises two divisions and typically yields four haploid daughter cells from one diploid cell.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q2). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Meiosis consists of how many divisions, producing how many daughter cells?
hints.rationale: Meiosis has two divisions and yields four daughter cells.

hints.prompt: Because meiosis reduces chromosome number, the daughter cells are:
hints.rationale: Meiosis is a reduction division, producing haploid cells.

hints.prompt: Why is 'two haploid' not the typical product of meiosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Meiosis has two divisions yielding four cells, not two

choices.label: B
choices.text: Meiosis produces only two cells in total

choices.label: D
choices.text: Meiosis is only a single division

hints.rationale: Two divisions produce four cells, so 'two' undercounts the products.

questions.stem: What structure is most important in forming the tetrads?
questions.explanation: The synaptonemal complex holds homologous chromosomes together during synapsis in prophase I, forming the tetrad (bivalent).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q3). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: During prophase I, homologous chromosomes are zipped together (synapsis) by the:
hints.rationale: The synaptonemal complex holds homologs together to form the tetrad.

hints.prompt: A tetrad (bivalent) is made up of how many chromatids from the paired homologs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Four chromatids (two homologs, each with two chromatids)

hints.rationale: A tetrad has four chromatids, formed once homologs are paired.

hints.prompt: Why is the 'chiasma' not the main structure that FORMS the tetrad?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A chiasma is the crossover point that appears within an already-formed tetrad, not what initially pairs the homologs

choices.label: B
choices.text: The chiasma zips the homologs together first

choices.label: C
choices.text: The chiasma is the microtubule organizer

hints.rationale: The chiasma arises after pairing; the synaptonemal complex is what forms the tetrad.

questions.stem: At which stage of meiosis are sister chromatids separated from each other?
questions.explanation: Homologous chromosomes separate in anaphase I; sister chromatids are not separated until anaphase II.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q4). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: In meiosis, homologous chromosomes separate in anaphase I; when do sister chromatids finally separate?
hints.rationale: Sister chromatids separate in anaphase II.

hints.prompt: What separates during anaphase I (meiosis I)?
hints.rationale: Anaphase I separates homologs, leaving sister chromatids joined.

hints.prompt: Why is 'anaphase I' not when sister chromatids separate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anaphase I separates homologous chromosomes; sister chromatids stay together until anaphase II

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anaphase I splits the sister chromatids apart

choices.label: C
choices.text: No separation occurs during anaphase I

choices.label: D
choices.text: Anaphase I is part of meiosis II

hints.rationale: Anaphase I separates homologs, so chromatid separation waits until anaphase II.

questions.stem: At metaphase I, homologous chromosomes are connected only at what structures?
questions.explanation: By metaphase I the synaptonemal complex has dissolved, and homologous chromosomes are held together only at the chiasmata (crossover points).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q5). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: By metaphase I, homologous chromosomes are held together at crossover points called:
hints.rationale: Chiasmata hold homologs together at metaphase I.

hints.prompt: By metaphase I, what has happened to the synaptonemal complex that earlier zipped the homologs?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has just formed for the first time

hints.rationale: The synaptonemal complex dissolves, leaving the homologs joined only at chiasmata.

hints.prompt: Why are 'kinetochores' not what connect the two homologs to each other at metaphase I?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Kinetochores attach chromosomes to spindle microtubules, not one homolog to another

choices.label: B
choices.text: Kinetochores physically link the two homologs together

hints.rationale: Kinetochores link chromosomes to spindle fibers, not homologs to each other.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not true in regard to crossover?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Spindle microtubules guide the transfer of DNA across the synaptonemal complex.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recombination nodules mark the crossover point.

questions.explanation: Crossover is mediated by recombination machinery at the synaptonemal complex, not by spindle microtubules. The other statements correctly describe crossing over.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q6). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Crossing over exchanges DNA between nonsister chromatids using recombination machinery located at the:
hints.rationale: Crossing over occurs via recombination machinery at the synaptonemal complex.

hints.prompt: Do spindle microtubules perform the DNA exchange during crossing over?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; spindle microtubules move whole chromosomes later, they do not transfer DNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; microtubules carry DNA across during crossover

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; microtubules are the recombination machinery

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only in mitosis do they transfer DNA

hints.rationale: Spindle microtubules move chromosomes; they do not carry out the DNA exchange.

hints.prompt: Since the question asks which statement is NOT true, why is 'nonsister chromatids exchange genetic material' not the answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That statement is true, so it cannot be the false (incorrect) one

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is false because sister chromatids do the exchange

hints.rationale: Nonsister chromatids really do exchange material, so this true statement is not the exception.

questions.stem: What phase of mitotic interphase is missing from meiotic interkinesis?
questions.explanation: Interkinesis (between meiosis I and II) lacks an S phase, so DNA is not replicated again before the second meiotic division.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q7). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Between meiosis I and II, no DNA is copied because interkinesis lacks which phase?
hints.rationale: Interkinesis lacks an S phase, so DNA is not re-replicated.

hints.prompt: If DNA were replicated again between the two meiotic divisions, the chromosome reduction would fail. Which synthesis step must therefore be skipped?
hints.rationale: Skipping DNA synthesis (S phase) preserves the reduction in chromosome number.

hints.prompt: Why is 'G1 phase' not the key phase whose absence prevents DNA re-replication?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the S (synthesis) phase, not G1, whose absence prevents DNA copying before meiosis II

choices.label: B
choices.text: G1 is where DNA is actually copied

choices.label: C
choices.text: Skipping G1 is what duplicates the DNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: G1 and S phase are the same phase

hints.rationale: DNA copying happens in S phase, so it is S phase, not G1, that is absent.

questions.stem: The part of meiosis that is similar to mitosis is ________.
questions.explanation: Meiosis II resembles mitosis: sister chromatids of haploid cells separate, just as in a mitotic division.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q8). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Which meiotic division separates sister chromatids, just as mitosis does?
hints.rationale: Meiosis II separates sister chromatids, resembling mitosis.

hints.prompt: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes. Is that like mitosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; mitosis separates sister chromatids, not homologs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; meiosis I is identical to mitosis

hints.rationale: Mitosis separates sister chromatids, so meiosis I (which separates homologs) is the unlike one.

hints.prompt: Why is 'anaphase I' not the meiotic part most like mitosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Anaphase I separates homologs, whereas mitosis (like meiosis II) separates sister chromatids

choices.label: B
choices.text: Anaphase I separates sister chromatids like mitosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Anaphase I is identical to a mitotic division

hints.rationale: Anaphase I separates homologs, unlike mitosis, so it is not the mitosis-like part.

questions.stem: If a muscle cell of a typical organism has 32 chromosomes, how many chromosomes will be in a gamete of that same organism?
questions.explanation: Gametes are haploid, containing half the somatic chromosome number: 32 / 2 = 16.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q9). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Gametes contain how many chromosomes compared with body (somatic) cells?
hints.rationale: Gametes are haploid, with half the somatic chromosome number.

hints.prompt: Would a gamete have MORE chromosomes than the 32-chromosome muscle cell (for example, 64)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; it has fewer, since gametes carry half the number

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; gametes double the somatic number

hints.rationale: Gametes have fewer chromosomes than somatic cells, ruling out 64.

hints.prompt: Why is '32' not the gamete's chromosome number here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 32 is the diploid somatic number; a haploid gamete has half of that

choices.label: B
choices.text: Gametes keep the full somatic number of 32

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gametes are diploid like muscle cells

hints.rationale: The gamete carries half of 32, so 32 itself is the somatic (not gamete) number.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes the genetic content of the two daughter cells in prophase II of meiosis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: haploid with one copy of each gene

choices.label: B
choices.text: haploid with two copies of each gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: diploid with two copies of each gene

choices.label: D
choices.text: diploid with four copies of each gene

questions.explanation: After meiosis I the cells are haploid, but each chromosome still consists of two sister chromatids, so each gene is present in two copies until anaphase II.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q10). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: After meiosis I is complete, each resulting cell is:
hints.rationale: Meiosis I reduces the cells to haploid.

hints.prompt: Even though the cell is haploid after meiosis I, each chromosome still consists of how many sister chromatids until anaphase II?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Two sister chromatids (so two copies of each gene)

choices.label: B
choices.text: One chromatid (one copy of each gene)

hints.rationale: Each chromosome retains two sister chromatids, giving two gene copies until anaphase II.

hints.prompt: Why is 'diploid with two copies of each gene' incorrect for prophase II?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Meiosis I already reduced the cell to haploid, so it is not diploid at prophase II

choices.label: B
choices.text: The cell is still diploid at prophase II

choices.label: C
choices.text: Meiosis I does not reduce ploidy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prophase II comes before meiosis I

hints.rationale: The cell is haploid (not diploid) by prophase II, even though each gene is present in two copies.

questions.stem: The pea plants used in Mendel's genetic inheritance studies were diploid, with 14 chromosomes in somatic cells. Assuming no crossing over events occur, how many unique gametes could one pea plant produce?
questions.explanation: With 14 chromosomes, the haploid number is n = 7. Independent assortment gives 2^n = 2^7 = 128 genetically unique gametes (ignoring crossing over).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q11). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified by calculation (2^7 = 128).

hints.prompt: Ignoring crossing over, the number of genetically different gametes from independent assortment is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2 raised to the haploid number n

hints.rationale: Independent assortment yields 2^n gamete combinations.

hints.prompt: A diploid pea plant with 14 chromosomes has a haploid number n of:
hints.rationale: Fourteen chromosomes means n = 7, so 2^7 gamete types are possible.

hints.prompt: Why is '28' the result of a wrong approach?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 28 comes from multiplying 14 by 2, not from raising 2 to the power of the haploid number

choices.label: C
choices.text: 28 is the correct count of unique gametes

hints.rationale: 28 is 14 times 2, not 2^n, so it reflects the wrong formula.

questions.stem: How do telophase I and telophase II differ during meiosis in animal cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cells remain diploid at the end of telophase I, but are haploid at the end of telophase II.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Daughter cells form a cell plate to divide during telophase I, but divide by cytokinesis during telophase II.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cells enter interphase after telophase I, but not after telophase II.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chromosomes can remain condensed at the end of telophase I, but decondense after telophase II.

questions.explanation: In many organisms the chromosomes stay condensed through telophase I (and brief interkinesis) and only fully decondense after telophase II. Cells are already haploid after telophase I, and animal cells divide by cytokinesis (cleavage furrow), not a cell plate.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q12). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: In many organisms, between meiosis I and meiosis II the chromosomes typically:
hints.rationale: Chromosomes often stay condensed through the brief interkinesis.

hints.prompt: After telophase I, are the daughter cells already haploid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes; homologs separated in meiosis I, so the cells are haploid

choices.label: B
choices.text: No; they remain diploid until telophase II

hints.rationale: Cells are haploid after telophase I, so any claim they stay diploid until telophase II is false.

hints.prompt: In animal cells, why is 'daughter cells form a cell plate during telophase I' incorrect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Animal cells divide by a cleavage furrow, not a cell plate (which is used by plant cells)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Animal cells always build a cell plate

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cell plates form during every telophase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Animal cells never divide their cytoplasm

hints.rationale: Animal cytokinesis uses a cleavage furrow, so the cell-plate statement is wrong for animals.

questions.stem: What is a likely evolutionary advantage of sexual reproduction over asexual reproduction?

choices.label: B
choices.text: There is a lower chance of using up the resources in a given environment.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sexual reproduction results in variation in the offspring.

questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction shuffles alleles (via independent assortment, crossing over, and fertilization), producing genetic variation that can aid adaptation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q13). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: What is the main evolutionary advantage of sexual reproduction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It generates genetic variation among offspring

choices.label: B
choices.text: It produces exact clones of the parent

choices.label: D
choices.text: It removes the need for meiosis

hints.rationale: Sexual reproduction's key benefit is genetic variation in offspring.

hints.prompt: Independent assortment, crossing over, and random fertilization all do what to alleles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Shuffle them into new combinations, increasing variation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Keep them in fixed, identical combinations

hints.rationale: These processes shuffle alleles, producing offspring variation.

hints.prompt: Why is 'sexual reproduction involves fewer steps' not a real advantage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sexual reproduction is actually more complex (meiosis plus fertilization), so 'fewer steps' is false

choices.label: B
choices.text: It genuinely uses fewer steps than asexual reproduction

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fewer steps is its primary benefit

hints.rationale: Sexual reproduction has more steps, so 'fewer steps' is not an advantage.

questions.stem: Which type of life cycle has both a haploid and diploid multicellular stage?
questions.explanation: Alternation of generations (seen in plants and some algae) includes both a multicellular haploid (gametophyte) stage and a multicellular diploid (sporophyte) stage.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q14). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: A life cycle featuring BOTH a multicellular haploid stage and a multicellular diploid stage is called:
hints.rationale: Alternation of generations has both multicellular haploid and diploid stages.

hints.prompt: In most animals, is there a multicellular HAPLOID stage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; only the single-celled gametes are haploid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; animals have a large multicellular haploid body

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; the adult animal is haploid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Animals have no haploid cells at all

hints.rationale: Most animals lack a multicellular haploid stage, so that is not the answer.

hints.prompt: Why is 'most fungal life cycles' not the best answer for having both multicellular haploid AND diploid stages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most fungi are predominantly haploid and lack a prominent multicellular diploid stage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fungi have equally prominent multicellular diploid bodies

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fungi have no haploid stage at all

hints.rationale: Fungi are mostly haploid without a major diploid multicellular stage, unlike alternation of generations.

questions.stem: What is the ploidy of the most conspicuous form of most fungi?
questions.explanation: Most fungi spend the majority of their life cycle in the haploid state; the conspicuous body is typically haploid.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q15). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Most fungi spend the majority of their life cycle as:
hints.rationale: Most fungi are predominantly haploid.

hints.prompt: In most fungi, is the diploid state typically brief or extended?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Brief, often only just after fertilization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Permanent for the whole life cycle

hints.rationale: The diploid phase is transient, so the conspicuous body is haploid.

hints.prompt: Why is 'diploid' not the ploidy of the conspicuous body of most fungi?

choices.label: A
choices.text: In most fungi the diploid phase is transient, so the visible body is usually haploid

choices.label: B
choices.text: The visible fungal body is normally diploid

hints.rationale: The diploid stage is fleeting, so the conspicuous form is haploid, not diploid.

questions.stem: A diploid, multicellular life-cycle stage that gives rise to haploid cells by meiosis is called a ________.
questions.explanation: The sporophyte is the diploid, multicellular stage that produces haploid spores via meiosis in organisms with alternation of generations.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q16). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: In alternation of generations, the diploid, multicellular stage that produces haploid spores by meiosis is the:
hints.rationale: The sporophyte is the diploid stage that makes spores by meiosis.

hints.prompt: Is the gametophyte stage haploid or diploid?
hints.rationale: The gametophyte is haploid, so it is not the diploid spore-producing stage.

hints.prompt: Why is 'gamete' not the diploid, multicellular stage described?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A gamete is a single haploid reproductive cell, not a diploid multicellular stage

choices.label: B
choices.text: A gamete is a diploid multicellular body

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gamete and sporophyte mean the same thing

hints.rationale: A gamete is a single haploid cell, unlike the diploid multicellular sporophyte.

questions.stem: Many farmers are worried about the decreasing genetic diversity of plants associated with generations of artificial selection and inbreeding. Why is limiting random sexual reproduction of food crops concerning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mutations during asexual reproduction decrease plant fitness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Consumers do not trust identical-appearing produce.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Larger portions of the plant populations are susceptible to the same diseases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Spores are not viable in an agricultural setting.

questions.explanation: Low genetic diversity means many plants share the same vulnerabilities, so a single disease can devastate a large fraction of the crop.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Biology 2e, Ch. 11 Meiosis and Sexual Reproduction — Review Questions
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax Biology 2e (Ch.11 review Q18). No public OpenStax answer key; answer verified from the Biology 2e source text.

hints.prompt: Low genetic diversity in a population means many individuals share:

choices.label: A
choices.text: The same vulnerabilities, such as susceptibility to a particular disease

hints.rationale: Low diversity means shared vulnerabilities across individuals.

hints.prompt: If a new disease strikes a genetically uniform crop, how much of it is at risk?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A large fraction, because they share the same vulnerability

hints.rationale: Shared vulnerability puts a large portion of a uniform crop at risk.

hints.prompt: Why is 'consumers do not trust identical-appearing produce' not the genetic concern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The real risk is biological vulnerability to disease, not consumer perception of appearance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Consumer distrust is the main genetic danger

hints.rationale: The concern is disease vulnerability from low diversity, not how produce looks to shoppers.
